questions.prompt: Which of the following antibodies are indicative of Type 1 Diabetes mellitus (T1DM)?

answer.explanation: Both GAD and Zn18 are antibodies associated with T1DM

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes pathophysiological changes in Type 2 Diabetes mellitus (T2DM)?

answer.explanation: GLP-1 decreases in patients with T2DM. GIP increases in patients with T2DM but they don’t produce insulin in response to the increase in GIP

questions.prompt: You recently diagnosed Mark, an 18-year-old patient with T1DM. He works shifts in a factory and frequently skips meals because of his work schedule. What would be the best MDI insulin regimen for him considering the nature of his work?

answer.explanation: A 4-dose MDI consisting of 3 fast-acting insulin doses before meals and a single long-acting would be best for reducing the risk of hypoglycemia in this patient. For example, if they have to skip a meal because of work, they can skip the insulin dose associated with that meal

questions.prompt: Which of the following is FALSE regarding the diagnosis of T1DM

options.label: a
options.text: Polyuria and polydipsia are diagnostic symptoms of T1DM

options.label: b
options.text: A random blood glucose of > 11.1 is sufficient for the diagnosis of T1DM in a symptomatic patient

options.label: c
options.text: You must do a fasting plasma glucose and/or HbA1c to confirm the diagnosis of T1DM in a symptomatic patient

answer.explanation: A patient who has symptoms of diabetes (such as polyuria and polydipsia) and a random BG of 11.1 can be diagnosed with diabetes. No confirmatory test is required in this case

questions.prompt: Which of the following is TRUE regarding T1DM and T2DM?

options.label: a
options.text: T1DM and T2DM are both caused by absolute insulin deficiency

options.label: b
options.text: T2DM often presents as an acute condition

options.label: c
options.text: Patients with T2DM are more likely to have other manifestations of metabolic syndrome

options.label: d
options.text: None of the above is true

answer.explanation: Patients with T2DM do not have an absolute insulin deficiency but they have insulin resistance. T2DM is less likely to present as an acute condition than T1DM. Patients with T2DM often have other features associated with metabolic syndrome

questions.prompt: Which of the following is TRUE regarding the risk of T2DM

options.label: a
options.text: A child born to a parent with T2DM has a higher risk of developing T2DM

options.label: b
options.text: If a twin develops T2DM, their monozygotic twin has a 90% risk of developing T2DM

options.label: c
options.text: The risk of developing T2DM is the same for monozygotic and non-monozygotic twins

options.label: d
options.text: A and B are both true

answer.explanation: Having a first degree relative with T2DM increases the risk of T2DM. Monozygotic twins have a 90% risk of developing T2DM

questions.prompt: A patient presents to the ER with polydipsia and polyuria that have been going on for several days. Blood glucose is 30 mmol. pH is 7.4. Creatinine is elevated and the patient is looking very sick. The patient’s mother has a history of T2DM. What is the most likely diagnosis?

answer.explanation: Given the patient’s symptoms, very high BG, normal pH and their family history of T2DM, HHS is the most likely diagnosis. There is nothing in the question that supports a diagnosis of a septic shock.

questions.prompt: A patient with T1DM presents to the ophthalmologist for retinal evaluation. Retinal exam reveals the presence of new blood vessels in the retina. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: a
options.text: The patient needs to be monitored closely as they have pre-proliferative retinopathy

options.label: d
options.text: The patient requires treatment for proliferative retinopathy and effective glycemic control.

answer.explanation: The presence of new blood vessels is indicative of proliferative retinopathy which requires treatment.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is TRUE regarding the findings of DCCT trial?

options.label: a
options.text: Intensive BG control is associated with a reduction retinopathy only

options.label: b
options.text: Intensive BG control is associated with a reduction in retinopathy and preventing worsening of existing retinopathy

options.label: c
options.text: Intensive BG control is associated with a reduction retinopathy, neuropathy, and microalbuminuria

options.label: d
options.text: Both B and C are true

answer.explanation: Refer to Dr. McManus’s module on Prevention of Diabetes Complications

questions.prompt: You recently diagnosed Sally who is 25 with T2DM. When would you start annual screening for microalbuminuria?

answer.explanation: According to Diabetes Canada guidelines, screening for proteinuria begins at diagnosis for patients with T2DM and 5 years after diagnosis for patients with T1DM. Sally has T2DM so you would start screening her at diagnosis. (Link: https://guidelines.diabetes.ca/cpg/chapter29#sec5)

questions.prompt: Which of the following is a benefit of SMBG (self-monitoring of blood glucose)?

options.label: a
options.text: Allows patients to determine if they’re hitting their glycemic control target

options.label: b
options.text: SMBG is needed to prevent hypoglycemia

options.label: c
options.text: It can guide decisions by clinicians

options.label: d
options.text: Only A and B are true

options.label: e
options.text: A, B and C are true

answer.explanation: Refer to the targets for glycemic control module

questions.prompt: Which of the following is TRUE regarding insulin needs in pregnancy?

options.label: a
options.text: Insulin needs remain constant throughout pregnancy

options.label: b
options.text: Insulin needs drop in the first trimester before rising again in the second and third trimesters

options.label: c
options.text: Insulin needs rise in the first and second trimesters before dropping in the third trimester

answer.explanation: Inuslin needs drop in the first trimester before rising again slowly in the second trimester and rising more rapidly in the third trimester

questions.prompt: A pregnant patient presents to your family medicine office at 25 weeks gestation for a routine visit. The patient’s random blood glucose is 12 mM after drinking 50g of glucose. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: b
options.text: Ask the patient to fast and then repeat the test with 75g of oral glucose instead of 50g

options.label: c
options.text: The patient has gestational diabetes (GDM). You should refer them to diabetes education and a dietitian.

answer.explanation: The patient’s BG is >7.8 mM/L after a 50g oral glucose tolerance test. This is sufficient for a diagnosis of GDM. No fasting 75g oral glucose tolerance test is required in this case

questions.prompt: Which of the following is TRUE regarding diabetes and pregnancy?

options.label: a
options.text: Both gestational diabetes (GDM) and pre-existing diabetes put the fetus at the risk of congenital malformations if uncontrolled.

options.label: b
options.text: GDM is not associated with a risk of congenital malformations, but it puts the pregnant patient at risk of premature birth, C-section and macrosomia among other things

options.label: c
options.text: GDM is associated with an increased risk of T2DM in the mother later in life.

options.label: d
options.text: Both B and C are true

answer.explanation: GDM is not associated with a risk of congenital malformations but it puts mother and fetus at risk of pregnancy complications

questions.prompt: How are long-acting insulin preparations able to have a long duration of action?

options.label: b
options.text: Due to first-pass metabolism in the liver

options.label: c
options.text: Due to their slow release from the hexameric form to the monomeric form

answer.explanation: Long-acting insulin preparations are designed to dissociate slowly from the stable hexameric form.

questions.prompt: Which of the following anti-hyperglycemic drugs is associated with the highest risk of hypoglycemia?

answer.explanation: Of the drugs listed here, SU’s have the highest risk of hypoglycemia

questions.prompt: A 65-year-old patient with T2DM presents to the ER with dehydration and a significantly elevated creatinine. Which of the following medications might have contributed to this patient’s symptoms?

answer.explanation: Given the patient’s symptoms (dehydration, renal failure..etc), it’s most likely that they’ve had polyuria and hypovolemia due to SGLT2 inhibitor use.

questions.prompt: A 30-year-old patient presents to your family medicine office with concerns regarding their weight. The patient meets the criteria for pharmacotherapy. As part of your general assessment, you run a patient health questionnaire (PHQ-9) and suspect that the patient might be suffering from depression. Which medication would you not prescribe to this patient?
Which of the following is TRUE regarding bariatric surgery for obesity management?

options.label: a
options.text: Sleeve gastrectomy reduces the stomach to 25% of its original size

options.label: b
options.text: Sleeve gastrectomy is often recommended for patients with a high preoperative risk

options.label: c
options.text: Lapgastric band is not routinely recommended

options.label: d
options.text: All of the above are true

answer.explanation: Refer to the Intro to Obesity module
